skill.name: review
skill.description: Runs a two-axis code review for changes since a specified Git reference. It compares the diff against repository standards and the originating issue, PRD, or spec.
skill.summary: Review code changes against documented standards and product requirements.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many external-command hits, but most are false positives from Markdown backticks around commands, file names, and headings. A real medium-risk issue remains because SKILL.md tells the agent to pass a user-supplied Git reference into git diff and git log commands without validation.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Git Reference Passed Into Commands
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the agent to accept a user-supplied fixed point and pass it through, then run git diff and git log with that value. This is legitimate review behavior, but it can become command injection if an implementation builds shell commands from untrusted input instead of passing arguments safely.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command strings and user-controlled revision source are explicit. The risk depends on the execution wrapper, so this is a medium-confidence injection concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives From Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: Most reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown formatting around examples, file names, headings, or command text. They are not executable code inside this skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is Markdown, and the flagged text appears as prose or inline code examples. No Ruby code, shell script, or executable wrapper exists in the analyzed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potential Shell Command Injection Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes commands containing a placeholder for a user-provided fixed point. Agents should validate the Git reference or pass it as an argv argument, not interpolate it into a shell string.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly described in the workflow. It is dangerous only when executed through shell interpolation, which is not shown in SKILL.md.

content.user_title: Review Changes Against Standards and Specs
content.value_statement: Code reviews often miss whether a change follows local standards and matches the original request. This skill separates those checks into two focused review tracks.
actual_capabilities: Reviews changes between HEAD and a user-specified Git fixed point.
Runs separate standards and specification review tracks.
Identifies local standards files such as AGENTS.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, and tool configs.
Uses issue references, passed paths, or spec files to locate product requirements.
Aggregates both review reports without merging their priorities.
limitations: Requires a valid Git reference before the review can start.
Depends on available standards documents and spec sources in the repository.
Does not replace test execution, type checks, or security scanning.
May need safe command handling for unusual branch or tag names.

use_cases.title: Review a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: Compare a branch against main and separate style issues from requirements issues.

use_cases.title: Check PRD Compliance
use_cases.description: Verify whether implementation work matches a linked issue, PRD, or specification file.

use_cases.title: Audit Work Before Handoff
use_cases.description: Summarize standards and spec concerns before another reviewer or maintainer takes over.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Branch Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review against the main branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current branch against main. Separate findings into Standards and Spec sections.

prompt_templates.title: Review Against Commit
prompt_templates.scenario: Review work since a specific commit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changes since commit abc123. Use repository standards and the related issue if you can find it.

prompt_templates.title: Review With Spec Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my changes against main using docs/specs/billing.md as the spec source.

prompt_templates.title: Release Readiness Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before merge or release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review HEAD against release-candidate. Report standards violations, missing requirements, scope creep, and the worst issue per axis.

output_examples.input: Review this branch against main with the linked issue as the spec.
output_examples.output: The result contains a Standards section with documented convention issues and a Spec section with missing or extra behavior.

output_examples.input: Review since commit abc123 using docs/prd.md.
output_examples.output: The result lists standards concerns separately from PRD compliance findings, then ends with finding counts and the worst issue.

output_examples.input: Review against main, but there is no spec.
output_examples.output: The result skips the Spec review, explains that no spec was available, and still reports Standards findings.

best_practices: Provide an exact branch, tag, or commit before starting the review.
Keep standards and spec findings separate when acting on the report.
Run tests and linters separately before relying on the review outcome.
anti_patterns: Do not use unvalidated user input in shell command strings.
Do not treat the Spec section as complete when no spec source exists.
Do not merge both review axes into one ranked list.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews code changes since a fixed Git point against repository standards and a spec source.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: No. It reads diffs and documents. Run tests, linters, and type checks separately.

faq.question: What happens if no spec exists?
faq.answer: The Spec review is skipped, and the final report states that no spec was available.

faq.question: Can it review pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the branch, commit, tag, or merge base that should define the comparison.

faq.question: Why are there two review axes?
faq.answer: A change can follow local standards while still missing the requested behavior, or the reverse.

faq.question: Is there a security concern?
faq.answer: Yes. Git references should be validated or passed safely as command arguments.
